title: JavaScript Runtime Core
description: Execution-model and language-behavior questions that come up often in interviews.

cards.question: What is the difference between microtasks and macrotasks in JavaScript?
cards.shortAnswer: Microtasks run before the next macrotask; Promise callbacks are microtasks and setTimeout callbacks are macrotasks.
cards.expectedAnswer: Microtasks are processed after the current call stack finishes and before the runtime moves to the next macrotask. Promise callbacks go into the microtask queue, while setTimeout callbacks go into the macrotask queue. That is why Promise handlers usually run before a zero-delay timeout.
keyPoints: microtasks run before the next macrotask
cards.learnMore:
A useful mental model is: finish the current stack, drain microtasks, then move to the next macrotask. That explains why Promise handlers often run sooner than people expect.

In interviews, it helps to connect this to responsiveness. Too many microtasks can still starve other work, so understanding the queue order matters for both correctness and performance.
commonTraps: saying setTimeout runs immediately with zero delay
mixing up call stack and queue ordering
followUps: How does this affect UI responsiveness?

cards.question: Is JavaScript pass-by-reference?
cards.shortAnswer: No. JavaScript is pass-by-value, and object references are themselves values.
cards.expectedAnswer: JavaScript is pass-by-value. For objects, the value being copied is the reference, not the object itself. That is why mutating an object through one variable is visible through another variable that points to the same object, but reassigning one variable does not change the other.
cards.learnMore:
This is one of those interview questions that sounds trickier than it is. The clean answer is: JavaScript always passes values. For objects, that value happens to be a reference-like pointer.

That is why changing object contents is visible through other variables that point to the same object, while reassigning the parameter only changes the local variable binding.
commonTraps: confusing shared object mutation with parameter passing semantics
followUps: How would you explain shallow copy vs deep copy after that?
Why does spread not solve every cloning case?
